data.title: Week Ahead: Hope Springs Eternal
data.description:
Hope that the ceasefire will lead to the end of the Middle East war fanned risk appetites in recent days. Equities rallied broadly. May WTI and June Brent fell by nearly 11.5%. The dollar fell against all the G10 currencies, the Antipodeans, and the Scandis, which seem the most sensitive to growth prospects and the risk environment. The JP Morgan Emerging Market Currency Index rose by a little more than 2% to post its best week since March 2018.

Negotiations will take place over the weekend, and market participants are hopeful that this can lead to a more sustained end to hostilities. Of course, it is complicated by the lack of trust and the Israel-Lebanon conflict. Ceasefires are hardly immaculate. Meanwhile, we may be on the cusp of two other watersheds. First, there have been encouraging reports about a potential agreement between Russia and Ukraine. Second, Hungary’s Viktor Orban is the longest-serving prime minister in the EU. He has served for the past 16 years and had a previous term between 1998 and 2002. Polls suggest he could lose the popular vote on April 12.

US

Drivers: The two-week ceasefire in the Middle East war reset the dollar and interest rate expectations. The dollar rallied during the war and was sold on the ceasefire. The derivatives market sees the Federal Reserve as the most dovish of the major central banks, and the odds of a cut this year have risen to around 33% from briefly discounting a small chance of a hike.

Data: The US has a full slate of high-frequency reports in the coming days. We suspect most will have limited impact on the markets. Yet, given the desire for real-time insight into the impact of the war, the Empire State survey and the Philadelphia Fed's survey may be the most important. The Fed's Beige Book and its anecdotal reports may also serve this purpose, though practically no one doubts that the FOMC will stand pat when it meets later this month.

Prices: The Dollar Index (DXY) gapped lower in the middle of last week. That gap, found between about 99.18 and 99.51, is important from a technical perspective. It found support around the 200-day moving average, found near 98.50. The momentum indicators have turned down, and the five-day moving average has fallen below the 20-day moving average for the first time since February 18. We look for the 97.50 area in the coming days.

EMU

Drivers: The euro bottomed in the middle of March, but the upside seemed stalled until the ceasefire was announced. Expectations for ECB rate hikes this year were pared from at least three hikes to two, with about a 40% chance of a third. The US two-year premium over Germany was around 138 bp before the war, and it collapsed to about 108 bp as President Trump threatened to destroy Iran. The ceasefire announcement saw it recover to almost 130 bp and it finished last week near 123 bp.

Data: With several large members already reporting national figures for February industrial output (Germany 0.5%, France -0.7%, Spain 0.2%) the aggregate figure will likely not draw much attention. The same could be said about the aggregate trade and current account figures. The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey of 0.3% growth in Q1 and Q2 this year seems a little exaggerated. Last month ECB projections saw growth at 0.9% this year and the current account surplus at 1.7% of GDP (1.9% in 2025), with a larger budget deficit (3.4% of GDP vs. 3.0% in 2025).

Prices: The euro appears to have forged a solid base in the second half of March. The consolidation of the past couple of sessions looks constructive. The five-day moving average is above the 20-day moving average, and the momentum indicators give the single currency scope for additional near-term gains. Nearby resistance is seen around $1.1745-50, but we suspect there may be initial potential toward $1.1825, the high from the last day in February, before the war began. Potential for a breakthrough in negotiations between Russia and Ukraine and a defeat of Hungary's Orban would seem supportive of the euro.

PRC

Drivers: Beijing continues to endorse the appreciation of the yuan. Last week, the PBOC set the dollar's reference rate at its lowest level in three years. The fix was lowered last week by about 0.30%, the largest weekly decline in two months.

Data: By the end of the week ahead, we will have been told Beijing's estimate of Q1 GDP and the macro details for March, including trade, retail sales, industrial production, investment, and house prices. The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey is that the economy grew at a slightly faster pace in Q1 than it did in Q4 25 (1.2%).

Prices: Ahead of the weekend, the dollar posted its lowest close against the offshore yuan since March 2023. A break of CNH6.8200 could signal a move toward CNH6.80, but there is potential toward the 2023 low, which was slightly below CNH6.70. It is difficult to know the intent of Chinese officials, but they have embraced the gradual appreciation of the yuan. We suspect a move to CNY6.60-CNY6.70 may be acceptable.

Japan

Drivers: The 30-day correlation between changes in the dollar-yen exchange rate and the 10-year US yield continues to hover above 0.60, the highest since last September. It bottomed in early February a little below 0.15, which was the lowest since last May. The 30-day correlation of changes in the dollar-yen exchange rate and the Dollar Index is a little above 0.75. At the end of February, it was near 0.73 but fell below 60 in mid-March. It has not been above 0.80 since last October.

Data: After a 4.3% month-over-month surge in Japan's industrial production in January, a monster of a gain, the largest since June 2022, it slowed by almost half in February. It is subject to revision in the week ahead. While the Japanese economy seems sufficiently strong and price pressures sufficiently persistent for the Bank of Japan to hike later this month, one more worrisome development has been the weakness in private sector machinery orders (capex). They fell by 5.5% in January. The February report is due on April 15.

Prices: The market tested the JPY160 level early last week, and the news of the ceasefire saw the dollar come off to support near JPY158.00. The market does not appear to have given up on probing for the pain threshold of Japanese officials, though the daily momentum indicators are falling and the five-day moving average in threatening to fall through the 20-day moving average for the first time since February 20. That said, a convincing break of JPY157.50 would suggest a high is in place.

UK

Drivers: Sterling continues to trade inversely to the Dollar Index. The 30-day rolling correlation of the changes of the two instruments is almost -0.87. It has not been much more extreme than this since early Q4 25. The correlation between the exchange rate and changes in two-year US and UK yields stands around -0.35 and -0.10, respectively. Meanwhile, the correlation between changes in sterling and the US S&P 500 (risk proxy) is a little below 0.57. This is the upper end of the range since late 2022.

Data: At the end of the week, the UK reports February GDP and details. Even though the monthly figures do not translate into quarterly reports as one might intuitively expect, the market is often sensitive to them. January GDP unexpectedly stagnated (median forecast in Bloomberg's survey was 0.2%). Another disappointment would likely spark sterling sales and a reconsideration of the likely trajectory of BOE. The central bank projects 0.9% growth this year and the Office for Budget Responsibility forecasts 1.1%. This may also prove to be optimistic.

Prices: Ahead of the weekend, sterling settled at its best level since the war began. Yet it did not make it above the intraday March highs (~$1.3480-85). The momentum indicators are constructive, and the five-day moving average pushed above the 20-day moving average. Above the March highs is the $1.3515 area, which is the halfway mark of the losses since the January 27 four-year high was reached (~$1.3670). A note of caution is that the upper Bollinger Band is slightly below $1.3500.

Canada

Drivers: Around mid-March, the 30-day correlation of changes in the US dollar-Canadian dollar exchange rate and the Dollar Index rose above 0.85, its highest since mid-2024. It is now near 0.65, which is still at the upper end of last year's range. The correlation between changes in the exchange rate and the S&P 500 is around -0.28, while the correlation with changes in the US two-year yield is slightly lower, though near the highs since last October.

Data: Canada housing data does not capture the market's attention, but the international transactions may be a somewhat different matter. After a weak H1 25, foreign demand for Canada's stocks and bonds picked up in H2 25 and continued into January. In fact, January's portfolio capital inflows were more than twice the Q4 25 average. On the other hand, the trade balance may be deteriorating. The Jan-Feb deficit of almost C$10 bln is among the largest Canada has ever recorded, and the rolling 12-month average shortfall of C$3.675 bln is a record.

Prices: Before the weekend, and despite a milquetoast jobs report that saw full-time jobs lost for the second consecutive month, the Canadian dollar rose to its best level in nearly two and a half weeks. The greenback was pushed below CAD1.38 and settled below the 20- and 200-day moving averages, which converged around CAD1.3820. The momentum indicators have turned down. The next target is around CAD1.3750, and then possibly CAD1.37.

Australia

Drivers: Changes in the Australian dollar and the Dollar Index are the most inversely correlated (30 days) since last October (~-0.78). The correlation between the exchange rate and the S&P 600 is around 0.64, which is off the peak seen last month near 0.73. Last year's high was closer to 0.87. The Aussie's correlation with gold peaked in February a little above 0.80. It fell to almost 0.30 in March before recovering to almost 0.50 now.

Data: At the end of the week, Australia reports March jobs data. In February, the almost 49k increase in employment was fueled exclusively by part-time positions. There were 30.5k fewer full-time jobs. However, that came after two solid months in which full-time posts rose by nearly 110k. The unemployment rate is 4.3%. It has spent only one month higher since the end of 2021, and that was the anomalous jump to 4.5% last September and a return to 4.3% in October. The Reserve Bank of Australia meets next on May 5. The futures market has about a 65% chance of a hike. We wonder if three hikes in a row are a bit aggressive given the hornet's nest that has been opened by the Middle East war.

Prices: The Australian dollar is knocking on the $0.7100 area, which it last traded above on March 19. Following the second rate hike of the year, the Aussie reached slightly beyond $0.7185 on March 13, its best level since mid-2022. The momentum indicators have turned higher from oversold, and the five-day moving average crossed back above the 20-day moving average last week. Initial resistance above $0.7100 is seen near $0.7125.

Mexico

Drivers: The Mexican peso is sensitive to the US dollar's broad direction. The rolling 30-day correlation of changes in the USD-peso exchange rate and the Dollar Index is above 0.75 for the first time since last October, which also marked the high in 2025. Yet the peso is also sensitive to the risk environment. Using the S&P 500 as the proxy, the USD-peso exchange rate is inversely correlated to changes in the S&P 500 by almost 0.80, the most extreme since July 2020. The correlation between changes in the exchange rate and US two-year yields is near 0.45, the highest since last September.

Data: While keeping in mind the large informal economy in Mexico, which accounts for a little over half of the workforce according to some estimates, nominal wage increases have been trending lower. The March figures are due this week. The rolling six-month moving average was slightly above 6.35% in February, the smallest since Q1 22.

Prices: The Mexican peso appreciated by about 3.3% against the US dollar last week. That is its best weekly showing since September 2024. The dollar settled near MXN17.2270 on the eve of the Middle East war and peaked in late March around MXN18.1645, slightly shy of the 200-day moving average. At the end of last week, it tested the MXN17.25 area, the lowest since March 2. The momentum indicators are falling, and the five-day moving average fell below the 20-day moving average last week for the first time since early March. However, the move has taken place so quickly that the greenback has settled for the past three sessions below the lower Bollinger Band (~MXN17.36 before the weekend).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Strattec: Profitability Transformation Is Driving The Story
data.description:
Strattec Security Corporation (STRT) is a stock that has continued to perform well since I first reviewed it. There have been some fluctuations but overall the stock has maintained a positive trend line. That is really due to the continued delivery and performance from the company. It continues to grow and has also increased profitability. The company continues to be a stock that seems to fly under the radar but produces results.

Last time I reviewed the stock there were some concerns about the auto market in general slowing down. While this has been the case, the company still showed growth and improved performance. It was able to grow despite the industry overall slowing down.

The stock recently pulled back from its highs and is sitting at a point where I think there continues to be value. The company has guided for a slowdown in the second half of the year, but the company has put itself in a position to be profitable despite a slowdown. It has also built a cash position of strength.

Financials

The company has continued to perform well. It really has shown growth and expansion across the board on the financial metrics. This has come despite concerns about the overall market demand provided at year end guidance for 2026 the company has been able to grow revenues year over year.

The company reported sales of $137.5 million for Q2, growth of 6% from the prior year. The revenue growth came despite the market slowdown that the company noted during year end. It was able to make up for a slower market by getting better pricing, favorable mix on content, new program launches, and tariff recovery. This is a real win for the company. Despite industry headwinds and a slower market the company was able to still grow revenues in a declining overall market. The concern here is if that is sustainable going forward.

The real transformation for the company has been the margins. The company reported an increase in gross profit of $5.6 million from the prior year. Gross margin for Q2 was 16.5% up by by 3.3% from the prior year. That is a huge increase that passes down to the bottom line. Much of this increase is due to pricing increases and product mix, but a big part of it is due to the restructuring and savings the company has been able to implement. This should carry forward and is not a one time bump. This can be seen as it has been 4 straight quarters that the gross margin has been over 16%. It does appear the gross margin gains are slowing down or stalling out. The company had a gross margin of 16.7% in Q4 and 17.3% in Q1. The latest quarter is down from the last 2 quarters. The company saw less headwind on the market side in Q1 which would contribute to higher sales and gross margin. Business will fluctuate and the margin is going to be affected by that, but I think there is a new higher baseline on the gross margin side of the business that will be maintained going forward.

This increase in margins passes through to overall profitability. Scale and leverage is starting to show up for the company in a significant way. Income from operations more than doubled from the prior year to reach $4.8 million, up from $2.1 million in the prior year. The first 6 months looks the same with an operating income of $15.3 million vs $7.2 million in the prior year. Adjusted EBITDA and Adj. EBITDA margin also increased significantly with the margin up by 2.8% over prior year. EPS nearly tripled to $1.20 from $0.32 in the prior year.

Lastly is the cash flow. The company reported cash from operations of $13.9 million up by $4.4 million, or 47% from the prior year. This has put the company in a position of strength on the balance sheet. It ended the quarter with $99 million in cash and equivalents, up from $90.5 million in Q1.

Restructuring and Business Transformation Costs

The company has been going through a restructure that has significantly improved margins and efficiency within the business. The leverage for the business can be seen when you look across the quarters. A couple extra million in revenue can push margins higher. A large part of this is due to the discipline that the company has taken in terms of its expenses. It runs an effective business to increase margins. A large part of this is due to the restructuring efforts that the company has taken over the past year.

The company announced their latest restructuring and cost initiative by offering an early retirement program and additional restructuring in their Mexico operations. It is expected to save $3.4 million annually from the changes. This should continue to help with the margins for the business, even with the expected slowdown in the second half of the year.

Selling, administrative, and engineering expenses have continued to become a larger percentage of revenues. This has been one concern of mine. In the latest quarter SAE expenses made up 13% of sales compared to 11.6% in the prior year. This has been creeping up for quite some time for the company. Management addressed this and said they expect this to be in the 10-11% range going forward. Without the one time voluntary retirement Q2 would have landed in that range during Q2.

One thing that I noticed around the restructuring is that the company has added an expense line, “Business Transformation Costs”. This expense line item has been growing rapidly and is starting to become impactful. The company also backs it out of adjusted EBITDA so the impact is not as noticeable. In the latest quarter this cost has almost reached $1 million dollars. What is the company spending $1 million on related to business transformation? There is a separate line item for “restructuring and similar charges”. The company notes in the 10-Q that they are going through a business transformation to “upgrade systems and processes, modernization of our support functions and a focus on productivity and efficiencies in our manufacturing operations”. This is a line item that is vague on details and is becoming a larger expense. I then read the press release, “Strattec Accelerates Strategic Transformation with New Brand Identity”. This made me think it is worse. The company is touting that it updated a website and how that “marks another milestone in Strattec’s transformation journey”. I have personally created and manage a couple of websites, the cost at this point in time is pretty small. I sure hope that $1 million is going a lot further than updating websites for a company that does not even sell to consumers. This seems like a consulting line item that is a bit out of control. This is not a big expense at the moment but it has been doubling each quarter. I will keep watching this item.

The other one that I noticed was the massive increase in SBC. It increased to $1.13 million in the latest quarter, an increase of more than 68% quarter over quarter. The company has been performing well and hitting milestones but that is quite an increase in SBC. I don’t want to see the gains for the company all being used up in SBC. This is also something I will be watching going forward.

Outlook

The company guided for a sales slowdown in the second half of the year, with an overall decline of 3 to 4 percent for the second half of the year. The company has been able to overcome the market slowdown in the first half of the year, but a big part of that was due to pricing benefits. The company will lap price increases implemented last year starting in Q3, so those benefits will start to disappear. This will make it much tougher for the company to continue to show growth despite the industry headwinds. That being said I do think the company will perform better than estimates, just as it did to start the year.

There is also an expected decrease in cash flow for the second half of the year. That is more related to some of the charges that will come with the early retirement plan. The company is also looking to build inventory a bit more. These are not big concerns. In the long run the savings from the early retirement package will pay off.

Management has positioned itself well for a market slowdown. The company has reduced costs and runs much more efficiently. Even if the market slows down the company can still generate a strong profit.

The one thing about the industry is that the company wins a contract for a vehicle and then it will provide the part for the vehicle for its life. That is typically 5-7 years before a company will remake the vehicle. This means it is really critical that Strattec get awarded during the design process to establish the continued business. The company has been focused on 2029 vehicle launches. It is critical that the company win those jobs now so that they are in line for the future production. Management brings it up each call and it is worth monitoring what management has to say about the sales cycle. Also look for any announcements around these wins. That establishes future revenue for the company.

The last thing to consider is the cash position. Management noted that this could be used to increase shareholder returns or also for future acquisitions. The company is not a high growth company but it is well established in its markets. I could easily see the company finding an acquisition to incorporate into its operations. This would provide growth for the company and utilize the growing cash.

Valuation

Strattec continues to carry a low valuation. The market cap sits at $343 million. It also has $99 million in cash with $2.5 million in debt. So the business itself is valued around $250 million. Trailing twelve month revenue was $586 million. That is a PS of .42 on EV/Sales. The industry average is 1.31. I understand that the business is not a high growth business. It does not necessarily deserve a premium valuation, but that is priced as if it is going to see a drop off in business. It should be noted that revenues are still expected to increase for the year as a whole.

That does not even take into account the growing profitability of the company. The company has been growing EPS by large percentages each quarter. The company trades at a PE of 12.47 well below the industry average of 20.4. From a valuation perspective it is very cheap compared to peers.

I do understand that sales are expected to slow in the second half of 2026. Also the company has a history of beating estimates. So I expect the slowdown to be less than what is guided. Overall for the year the company does expect to see an increase in revenues. Analysts do expect growth in revenues and earnings to slow down in the coming years, but regardless growth is expected along with continued profitability.

Conclusion

Strattec continues to perform. Despite there being industry headwinds the company grew revenues during the first half of the year. The real gain has come from the company restructuring its business and increasing profitability. The company has now reached a point where it is generating strong cash flow each quarter. It has also positioned itself for continued success by running a much more efficient business.

The company does not run at high growth rates and the growth is expected to slow down a bit more over the second half of the year. Despite this the company has been able to increase profits and cash flows. I would expect the company to continue to do the same going forward. The company has also built a strong cash position that can be utilized to increase shareholder return, or used for acquisitions and growth.

Strattec is not a high growth stock for your portfolio. It has been a steady performer. This has resulted in the stock outperforming the market. The company continues to trade at a low valuation. The stock is not jet fuel in your portfolio, but I think it will continue to outperform. I maintain my buy rating.

data.title: Vishay Intertechnology: Be Cautious When A Stock Goes Vertical
data.description:
Vishay Intertechnology (VSH), a manufacturer of discrete semiconductors and passive electronic components, is off to a great start in 2026 with the stock having gained 55.1% YTD. This after back-to-back years of losses for VSH in terms of its stock price, which coincided with poor quarterly results as a consequence of soft demand, including another net loss in the recently concluded FY2025.

This decline in demand was the result of prevailing market conditions. For instance, according to data from World Semiconductor Trade Statistics or WSTS, which tracks global semiconductor sales, the market for discrete semiconductors shrank in both 2024 and 2025. Companies needed less of the types of chips provided by VSH and this affected sales and profits by extension.

However, the market seems to be banking on much improved quarterly earnings in the new fiscal, which would explain the ongoing rally in VSH. Yet it is possible the market is expecting too much from VSH because growth could disappoint, something that would not be so unusual considering all that has happened.

VSH Is Soaring Higher

A previous article from November 2025 written by myself mentioned how a recent reversal in the stock, which saw a rally turn into a selloff, did not happen at no particular time because it occurred as soon as VSH came into contact with a trendline that had essentially forced the stock downwards for quite some time. VSH was in a downtrend as long as this trendline was intact and I therefore opted to rate VSH a hold in the article.

Almost half a year has passed and the chart above shows how VSH was able to break through the aforementioned trendline, which allowed for VSH to break the downtrend that was in place going all the way back to 2023. VSH was able to accomplish this with a strong rally in the last several months, although there was a correction along the way, which caused a temporary retreat.

In fact, VSH has been red-hot in recent days with the stock going almost vertical since March 30 when it closed at $16.56. VSH closed at $22.47 on April 10, which means VSH has appreciated its market cap by 35.7%, or more than a third, in less than two weeks to $3,062M. It needs mentioning for proper context that VSH was not alone in terms of rallying higher during this time. The entire semiconductor sector did as well.

For instance, the iShares PHLX Semiconductor ETF (SOXX), which tracks companies active in the semiconductor industry, gained about 24.8% during this time. Still, VSH is up 55.1% YTD, most of it coming in the past two weeks or so, which means VSH has not only outperformed most semis, but also the leading indexes like the SPDR $&P500 ETF (SPY).

Why The Rally May Be Facing A Hurdle Soon

However, such a large appreciation in such a short time is fraught with risk. For starters, VSH is now well into overbought territory with an RSI reading in the mid seventies. Furthermore, the stock has set a new 2026 and 52-weeks high of $22.47 by replacing the previous one of $22.00 set on February 4. The stock proceeded to go down in the following weeks and something similar might happen again. The chart below of the past 12 months shows how VSH has been active recently.

The decline in February does not seem to have been by accident. Recall how in the chart posted earlier, VSH went from a high of $30.10 in July 2023 to a low of $10.35 in April 2025, following the stock market selloff after “Liberation Day”. VSH has moved higher since then and it appears resistance is looming ahead because the 61.8% Fibonacci retracement of $30.10 to $10.35 is $22.56.

This is not only $0.56 above $22.00 or where the stock topped out in February before falling back, but it is also just $0.09 away from $22.47, or where the stock last closed at. Remember also that resistance at the 61.8% Fibonacci retracement level tends to be heavily defended by shorts. Combine this together with the stock being overbought and resistance might just put a halt to the furious rally of the last several days, if not send the stock tumbling south, just like what happened in February.

VSH Has Struggled With Soft Demand, But Better Is Expected

The rally is all the more noteworthy because it happened even though the most recent or Q4 FY2025 report was not exactly a blowout quarter as one might expect from the way the stock has been jumping higher, but more like a mixed bag with some good and some not so good. VSH actually missed earnings expectations in the Q4 FY2025 report, which means all four quarterly reports in FY2025 missed earnings expectations.

VSH reported GAAP earnings of $1.0M or $0.01 a share, or $0.01 less than expected, on revenue of $800.9M. In comparison, VSH recorded a net loss of $66.3M or $0.49 a share on revenue of $714.7M in Q4 FY2024, but keep in mind that this includes a goodwill impairment to the tune of $66.5M. In terms of EBITDA, which leaves out a number of items, the numbers look better with VSH reporting EBITDA of $70.3M in Q4 FY2025, much better than negative $0.3M of one year ago.

If all the fiscal quarters are added up, then VSH reported a GAAP net loss of $9.0M or $0.07 per share on revenue of $3,069.0M in FY2025. In comparison, VSH recorded a net loss of $29.8M or $0.23 a share on revenue of $2,937.6M in FY2024. FY2025 EBITDA was $275.5M, which was up from $210.3M in FY2024.

It is important to note here that the YoY gains were assisted by the low base of FY2024. FY2024 saw a huge drop in quarterly results, which allows for favorable comps in FY2025. If we were to ignore FY2024, then the FY2025 numbers are actually worse than those of FY2023. It’s also worth mentioning that VSH has made acquisitions during this time, which is skewing the YoY comparisons.

VSH spent $273.3M on capex in FY2025, less than the $320.1M in FY2024, but it still resulted in negative free cash flow of $87.8M. In contrast, free cash flow was negative $143.4M in FY2024 when VSH spent more on capex. The balance sheet got worse as a result with cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments declining to $515.2M at the end of FY2025, down from $606.4M at the end of FY2024, which was also more than offset by $1.07B of total debt.

The balance sheet will get worse because FY2026 capex is projected to come in at $400-440M, which is almost certain to result in another year of negative free cash flow. However, this was countered by the prospect of much stronger growth in the income statement. In fact, while no hard numbers were issued, VSH stated in the earnings call:

“In summary, 2026 is our year to take off.”

This was backed by a book-to-bill of 1.2 in Q4 FY2025, which is significant considering how much time VSH has dealt with a book-to-bill of below one in recent quarters. VSH received more new orders than what was billed and shipped out, which means revenue can be expected to increase in upcoming quarters when those booked orders are fulfilled. The extra production capacity that VSH added could come in handy here. Guidance calls for revenue of $800-830M in Q1 FY2026, an increase of 14% YoY at the midpoint compared to Q1 FY2025 when revenue was $715.2M.

Why VSH Could Be Risky At This Time

It may be tempting to jump in with the way the stock has spiked higher, but there is an argument to be made against doing so, besides the potential resistance and overbought conditions mentioned earlier. For starters, VSH has a high hurdle to cross if it is to live up to growth expectations that the recent surge in the stock price has essentially priced in. The stock closed at $22.47, which gives VSH a market cap of $3,062M and an enterprise value of $3,612M due to there being much more debt than cash.

TTM EBITDA totaled $275.5M, which translates to EV/EBITDA of 13.1x. According to the methodology favored by Peter Lynch, VSH needs to grow earnings at a CAGR of 13.1% in the next 3-5 years in order to be deemed fairly valued relative to growth. Revenue would have to grow at a similar pace, unless margins increase in which case VSH can afford a slower pace of revenue growth for earnings to hit the desired growth rate.

The issue here is that this growth is much faster than what VSH has been able to achieve in recent years. Revenue, for instance, has only grown at a CAGR of 4.17% in the past five years and that is with the aid of acquisitions. It is of course possible for VSH to grow faster in the coming years than it did in previous years, but here too there is reason for doubt.

VSH itself has stated that it agrees with projections calling for the market segments it serves to grow in the mid to high single digits. This means that if VSH only grows in line with the market, revenue would not grow fast enough and it would have to improve its margins if earnings are to grow in the double digits. Otherwise, earnings will not grow fast enough. The alternative would be for VSH to increase its market share by grabbing sales from competitors, which would allow for growth faster than the overall market. Both are not impossible, but neither will be easy to achieve.

Why Expected Growth Might Disappoint

The reason why VSH has spent so much on capex is to increase production capacity, in line with the goals laid out as part of the plan Vishay 3.0. VSH has increased production capacity based on the expectation that semiconductor demand will grow in the coming years, which will need increased production capacity to fulfill.

However, while demand has grown recently, its been rather uneven. On the one hand, demand driven by AI has been extremely strong, as is demand from defense applications. On the other hand, other end markets like automotive have been much weaker. Defense is likely to remain strong due to the tense global environment the world finds itself in, but AI is more of a question mark.

For instance, there are reports that up to half of all AI datacenter projects could face delays or even cancellation in 2026, which is up from a quarter in 2025. The reasons for these delays are multitude, including component shortages. However, a more serious one that could affect the rollout of datacenters is that there is increasing pushback from people living in places where these datacenters are supposed to be build.

For instance, one of the more commonly cited complaints is that datacenters, especially the gigantic ones favored by the hyperscalers, consume so much electrical power that they make it much more expensive for everyone else. The significance of this is that the AI boom has revolved heavily around the building of these massive datacenters, which has in turn greatly increased semiconductor demand, including the discrete chips supplied by VSH.

If the building of datacenters slows down, semiconductor demand is very likely to be impacted. AI demand has been very strong, but it is not impossible that demand may be much less in the coming years. This would adversely affect VSH, especially since AI demand has been the star performer for not just itself, but for many other semis.

Investor Takeaways

There is no question that VSH has been on fire lately. The stock closed at $16.56 as recently as March 30, but it has since gone almost vertical to close at $22.47 on April 10, an increase of $5.91 or 35.7% in less than two weeks. Many other semis have also gone on rallies during this time, but VSH has outperformed in large part because VSH itself has called for 2026 to be the year growth takes off.

This after an extended period in which soft demand caused growth to evaporate and the numbers to dip, which resulted in back to back net losses in FY2024 and FY2025. VSH had also started to initiate a plan of expansion, which caused the balance sheet to deteriorate due to heavy capex spending. Not surprisingly, the stock fell during most of this time.

Recent quarterly results do suggest demand is getting better than what it was for much of the past two years or so. Book-to-bill, which was below one for a big portion of the past two years, is now at 1.2, which bodes well for upcoming quarterly revenue, and earnings by extension. The general outlook is that growth will be much better in the short term than it has been in recent quarters.

However, there are no guarantees growth will live up to the elevated expectations that accompany a much higher value for the stock. VSH has actually grown much slower than it needs to in the coming years to warrant the growth that has been priced in. Growth is likely to be faster than recently, but the market may have overestimated how much growth there actually will be, going by how much the stock has spiked higher.

If earnings growth comes up short, it will be in line with what has happened before because VSH missed earnings estimates in all four quarters of the recently concluded FY2025. Moreover, it’s worth keeping an eye on what happens in the AI space. AI has been the standout performer for the semiconductor industry by boosting demand, but AI is increasingly having problems on different fronts.

This includes the boom in datacenters that is causing all sorts of issues that need to be resolved. If AI slows down, this will impact not just VSH, but the entire industry. It would also come at a bad time with the way VSH has leveraged the balance sheet. VSH has sacrificed the balance sheet for a shot at growing the income statement by banking on increased demand. This may pay off, but VSH is also taking a big risk if all the extra capacity added remains underutilized because demand did not take off as expected.

It may be tempting to ride the stock higher, but I am neutral on VSH because of all the risk that one needs to accept to take part in the rally. Whether it is the uncertainty as to whether VSH can grow earnings fast enough to warrant the surge in the stock price, the question marks as to how durable the AI boom is, the ongoing deterioration in the balance sheet or the resistance the stock is closing in on with VSH very much overbought, standing pat looks like the more prudent move at this time.

data.title: All Eyes On Cencora, A Healthcare Supplier That Could See Lots More Upside
data.description:
A Healthcare Supplier Eyeing The Competition

Seeing is believing, and recently I saw a late-March piece in Reuters on healthcare distributor Cencora (COR) buying out the retina-care business of EyeSouth for $1.1B, so I thought this would be a great time to do a followup article on this company.

One can quickly see from the company website that they have a diversified portfolio spanning many global regions as well as clinical needs including distribution of generics and biosimilars, hospital and pharmacy solutions, and OTC product distribution, among other things.

Since my last coverage almost a year ago, when I called this stock a buy, it is up around +12.2% since then, indicating market confidence and my accurate forecast. In particular, what I liked about them at the time was a favorable macroeconomic demand for healthcare supplies, as well as share price targets.

This time around, I'll previewing the stock again ahead of its upcoming May 6th earnings call and will look at some key points from their last results.

A Thesis Reaffirming the Prior Buy Rating

Based on my updated research, I reaffirmed my prior buy rating from 2025, as Cencora had several strong fundamentals across my 8 rating categories, despite there also being some technical indicators showing the share price retreating.

In this scenario, I am inclined to call it a dip-buying opportunity, taking advantage of the price retreat on an otherwise fundamentally strong company.

A Macro & Sector Outlook Driven By Growing But Very Specific Clinical Needs

In first considering the wider picture in which this company finds itself, I am highly bullish in this section, for a few key reasons.

I'll admit this sector can often sound very scientific, complex, or overly medical, for those of us not in that field, so I always try to simplify the discussion for brevity and what could drive upside or downside for investors.

Unlike pure-play big pharma stocks, Cencora sits in a unique niche and from its last earnings deck we get a sense that it is primarily a distributor, with some other ancillary business segments.

Some favorable forecasts in this niche were published in another major financial news site, with research firm SNS Insider saying the healthcare distribution market size is expected to keep "growing at a CAGR of 6.65% over 2026-2035," adding that this is driven by "demand for pharmaceuticals, biologics, and medical devices in healthcare systems around the globe."

I think that type of future scenario could continue driving tailwind for firms like Cencora, but so can its announcement in December that it will acquire remaining ownership in OneOncology, a platform that is "supporting patient access to high-quality care in local communities."

Back in January, for instance, a journal from the American Cancer Society forecasted that "in 2026, approximately 2,114,850 new cancer cases and 626,140 cancer deaths are projected to occur in the United States," but also struck a positive tone, saying "decades of scientific investment have translated to longer lives for people with even the most fatal cancers."

So, I believe the focus on both early detection and prevention, but also optimal treatment and management when needed, not only for cancers but also various other clinical indications, could continue to provide a natural role for a major brand like Cencora to serve the needs of both patient and practitioner.

Strong Competitive Positioning Driven By Metrics as Well as Global Penetration

Moving on, I considered Cencora's competitive positioning and here too I am highly bullish, driven by both historic performance vs peers and also qualitative factors.

To add an additional angle since my last article on Cencora, this time around I picked 3 peer comparables that fall into the niche of healthcare distributors, and will compare some metrics in this group: McKesson (MCK), Cardinal Health (CAH), and Henry Schein (HSIC).

Peer data shows Cencora pretty much tied for 1st place with McKesson in top line growth, with a 5-year revenue CAGR of +10.86%, while Cencora also lead in return on equity with a RoCE of +152%, so it seems to dominate both on the top and bottom lines, historically of course.

There was more good news for Cencora investors in terms of momentum data which showed Cencora and McKesson both beating a major benchmark like the S&P500 in terms of 10 year price return, so one could say they achieved "alpha" in the last decade, and I think it further strengthens any bullish case as it is a proven case over many market cycles.

On the qualitative side, besides its acquisitions in the retina care and oncology space I would highlight Cencora's global reach as a key competitive positioning factor, and an example would be its role in the European market where it says it delivers 2MM units of medicines and products daily just in the UK alone.

A Company Growth Trajectory via Acquisitions and Regional Demand

The company-specific growth factors seemed to remain positive, and so I gave this section a highly bullish rating as well.

Business acquisitions can drive future revenue and portfolio expansion, and so the 2 acquisitions I mentioned earlier could be considered an upside factor.

Beyond that, since my last coverage of Cencora, it has considerably made investments to grow its US distribution network. For instance, in November it announced "plans to invest $1B through 2030 to bolster and expand its pharmaceutical distribution network in the United States."

To further complement this growth-driven case, the company's own guidance for FY26 expects +7 to +9% growth of the top line, according to the fiscal FY26 Q1 investor deck (pg. 11) from February, which I think should further drive investor and analyst confidence in this stock.

One of its fiscal Q1 key performers I would highlight is the International Healthcare Solutions segment, where "revenue increased 9.6% to $7.6B on an as-reported basis, primarily due to growth in our European distribution business," according to the same investor deck (pg. 8).

This ties nicely into what I said earlier and that is that Cencora has already proven it can achieve competitive positioning on a global scale, which I think should continue to be complementary to the growth story.

Earnings Outlook Impressive, Despite Razor Thin Operating Margins

Next, when considering what could impact earnings, I am only modestly bullish, after considering the type of business and its margins, past performance, and future forecasts.

The margin of choice I would pick to compare in this peer group would be the operating margin, as it could be a stronger indicator of core business earnings efficiency, and in this case this sector appears like a relatively low-margin business type, ranging from 1-6% margins:

I point it out as I think investors should expect more of the same going forward, a business impacted by razor-thin margins, and Cencora seems to be rather in line with key peers McKesson and Cardinal.

Despite the razor-thin operating margin under 2%, in considering patterns, Cencora stilll managed to grow operating income two years in a row, according to income statement data:

Unlike big pharma who may see huge capex costs from ongoing R&D costs to grow their drug pipeline, here what investors should expect is think margins impacted by the economics of being a distributor: managing a logistics network and infrastructure, warehousing, dependence on high volumes, and limited leeway on prices. It is for this reason that a continually profitable company like Cencora really stands out.

The consensus also seems confident too, giving this stock 8 upward revisions (as of Saturday's data) and expecting 10-11% YoY EPS growth in FY26 and FY27, so I expect this to also fuel the bulls in the near term.

Balance Sheet Risk Shows Bright Spots, Despite Elevated Leverage

When it comes to balance sheet risk, after considering the peer comparison debt-to-equity, major credit ratings, but also cashflow strength from the core business, I am in a modest bullish zone this time.

First, I continue to see a high D/E just like in my prior coverage, indicating Cencora is quite leveraged, and actually makes it an outlier in this peer group, perhaps increasing the risk needle somewhat.

While a D/E above 4.0 may at first glance seem particularly elevated, it did not hurt Cencora's credit ratings, with S&P Global giving it an investment-grade BBB+ in its February note, saying their rating "reflects Cencora's strong market position as one of the three largest U.S. drug distributors."

Fitch was even more optimistic, having given Cencora an A- rating back in its December note, highlighting that "Cencora maintains a solid liquidity position."

I believe that in cases of high leverage like this, a proven strong cashflow from the core business is crucial, and from Cencora's cashflow data we can see that the company consistently has positive operating cashflow. So, for the upcoming earnings results I will be looking for a continuation of this trend, since any downward trend in positive cashflow could have a major downside impact.

A Proven Dividend Grower With Low Payout Ratio

When thinking about dividend yield, growth, and sustainability, the latter two really dominate with this stock and justify a bullish outlook.

This is a low-yield stock, with a fwd yield at just 0.75%, below its sector median, and not on my list of high-yield stocks.

However, what it lacks in yield it makes up for in proven dividend growth through many cycles, despite having razor-thin margins. Consider that it achieved a 10-year dividend CAGR of +6.2%, beating its sector median.

To visualize it, this is just the type of trend line I am looking for, and I think many investors would appreciate this pattern too:

Dividend data also shows a payout ratio around 13.7%, so it seems there is plenty of buffer between profits and dividend payouts, with over 3/4ths of earnings remaining. This could come in handy to help pay down a lot of that debt, while still growing the dividend.

If the positive EPS growth forecasts get met or exceeded, it can further support continued dividend safety and growth.

A Reasonably Valued Stock With Further Upside Signals

Here, I put together a valuation worksheet using Seeking Alpha data as inputs, and from my findings I am highly bullish when comparing the stock's valuation vs future upside potential.

What the above worksheet tells me in a simplified way for the sake of brevity and clarity is that Cencora is only slightly undervalued in fwd P/E, and is not a major outlier there. The P/B multiple is more elusive and skewed, and perhaps less reliable, as some of the peers have negative book values it seems.

Keep in mind that what could justify such confidence in future earnings, in a business with such thin operating margins, is that it is not impacted by seasonal or recessionary factors in the same way as some sectors might, and I showed earlier that the company has proven it can consistently be profitable despite thin margins.

Further, my share price forecast for Dec. 2026 expects a +24% upside. This is actually close to the average Wall St forecast of +25% upside (as of Saturday data), so in both cases it would justify a highly bullish view.

Technical Patterns Have Broken a Long Bull Run

In this final section, which also adds some more color to the overall rating, I considered some technical patterns in the chart below and gave the stock a neutral rating here.

What this chart pattern seems to show is that this stock has not had any clear buy or sell signals over the last year, such as golden crosses or death crosses, but what seems like a steady bullish trend, followed by a sudden reversal to the downside in the last few months.

Because this seems to coincide with the recent market downturn driven by geopolitical conflicts/market shock, it may not yet be a truly bearish trend yet since the company has otherwise strong fundamentals and could actually present a dip opportunity. For these conflicting reasons, I would be more neutral here.

Risks To a Bullish View Could Further Erode Thin Margins

A key risk to my bullish thesis that I did not include in my rating score could be the fact that this business is not only in a sector impacted heavily by regulation, but also by price pressure from healthcare payers (insurers, Medicare), and cost-control pressure from customers (pharmacies, hospitals, etc.). There is also the legal risk of drug and supply recalls, contaminations, defects, and so on, along with the role of the FDA as a watchdog.

So, it seems like Cencora is sandwiched among all of these risks at once that could cause headwinds to the bulls, but if I could pick just one that I think may have the biggest impact it would be the pricing pressure since this is already a low margin business to start with.

Conclusion: Agree With Wall St Buy Rating

In this updated article on Cencora, I ended up back at a buy rating again, and agreeing with the latest Wall St consensus as of this writing.

Despite some bearish-looking chart patterns lately, strong fundamentals push this stock over the edge for me into bull range, and in particular growth via acquisitions, and a globally diversified business.

For my investor readers, I would consider this stock for your watchlist of dividend ideas for April to have a second look at, as well as a growth idea too, while also acknowledging the fundamental risk of it being a low margin business.

data.title: Microsoft: Azure Is Booming, But OpenAI And Copilot Are Quietly Capping The Upside
data.description:
Performance Assessment

Microsoft (MSFT) has lagged the broader market index since my last update on the stock:

Elevator Pitch

  1. OpenAI's market share loss can hit MSFT's valuations
  2. Azure grows from booming cloud infrastructure demand, but monetization may be impaired
  3. Copilot adoption is below market expectations
  4. Multiple signs suggest a fair MSFT valuation
  5. Bulls and bears seem evenly matched

OpenAI's Market Share Loss Can Hit MSFT's Valuations

Microsoft has a 27% stake in OpenAI (OPENAI), valued at $852B. This puts the value of MSFT's stake in OpenAI at $230B, making up 8.3% of MSFT's overall market capitalization of $2.8T.

The problem is that ChatGPT's website and app market share has gradually been falling, especially since H2 CY25:

Already, people are realizing that the value of using multiple generative AI models together can get better outputs. For example, Perplexity has launched Model Council for users to utilize various models together:

Every AI model has blind spots. It might overlook context, lean toward certain perspectives, or fill gaps with confident guesses. For research you're acting on, it's a big risk.

Model Council builds on Perplexity’s advantage of using multiple models. Instead of switching between models, you can now query several at once and get one combined answer. When the models converge, you can move faster with confidence; when they disagree, you know how to dig deeper.

- Perplexity Blog

Based on these trends, I expect the AI chatbot market to be oligopolistic rather than an eventual winner-takes-all situation. Hence, I expect the first-mover advantage that OpenAI's ChatGPT has to continue eroding over time. This can be a headwind to valuation narratives on OpenAI and hence Microsoft's stake.

Azure Grows From Booming Cloud Infrastructure Demand, but Monetization May Be Impaired

Cloud infrastructure demand is booming as enterprise AI adoption ramps up:

As enterprise AI adoption increasingly centers on agents, workflows, and data integration, organizations require infrastructure environments that can be effectively orchestrated, scaled, and governed. This reinforces the role of cloud platforms as the operational foundation for AI

- Omdia Research (TTGT)

This is very good for Azure growth, which I expect will continue at similar rates of growth or even accelerate, especially as many organizations (particularly SMBs) are still in the early AI adoption phases:

The one caveat, however, is that Microsoft is using a lot of the compute for its own internal purposes rather than monetizing it from customers. Of course, they would ideally like to do both, but there are capacity constraints preventing this. This is impairing some revenue growth realization, and I expect a similar 200-300bps of lower growth realization to continue:

Microsoft noted it is diverting compute for internal capacity instead of focusing on revenue-generating Azure. The company noted this last quarter that it would have grown over 40% year-over-year (in constant currency) but grew 38% year-over-year due to these internal capacity shifts.

- BofA, Seeking Alpha News

Copilot Adoption is Below Market Expectations

I've generally had an optimistic view on Microsoft Copilot adoption trends, having discussed this multiple times in my past coverage. But recently, after finding out that the penetration rates of Copilot adoption are a low 3.5% of Office 365 seats, I am questioning the bullish narrative around this thesis point. This is augmented by the fact that some other sell-side analysts are also recognizing that Copilot growth needs to improve for the stock to genuinely re-rate higher:

narrative around M365/Copilot needs to improve in order for the stock to really re-rate higher

- UBS Analyst Karl Keirstead

To Microsoft's credit, however, the company seems to be making greater strides to drive AI-product-led growth with the launch of Copilot Cowork, a new AI for Enterprises bundle via the Microsoft 365 E7, and organizational leadership structure changes that enable greater focus on AI model innovations.

Multiple Signs Suggest a Fair MSFT Valuation

MSFT's 1-yr fwd PE of 21.3x is right near the comps' median of 22.2x:

The stock's valuation discount vs. peers of 4.2% is not too far off the longer-term typical discount level of 6%:

And the current 1-yr fwd PE is also right at the longer-term average of 20.8x:

All these multiple perspectives suggest that MSFT is probably near fair valuation right now.

Bulls and Bears Seem Evenly Matched

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return, as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

MSFT Technical Analysis

I see a clash of bullish and bearish forces on the monthly MSFT stock chart. On the bullish side, I see the prices are at a monthly support level. On the bearish side, I see strong bearish momentum. So on balance I do not have a strong net directional bias.

Takeaway & Positioning

  • Fundamental Thesis: Neutral, as strong Azure growth is offset by weaker Copilot adoption and potential OpenAI valuation de-ratings
  • Valuation: Neutral, as MSFT seems fairly valued
  • Technical Analysis: Neutral, as the bulls and bears seem to be in balance
  • Final Assessment and Rating: Neutral/Hold

data.title: Thoughts From The Municipal Bond Desk
data.description:
By Mark Paris, Chief Investment Officer, Invesco Fixed Income, Municipals | Tim Spitz, Senior Client Portfolio Manager, Team Lead

Tim: March was a tough month for municipal bonds, with the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index returning -2.32%. 1 What do you think accounted for the weakness?

Mark: I think it was due to several challenges that hit all at once. Rising geopolitical tensions drove oil prices higher, spurring renewed inflation worries. As inflation expectations increased, investors demanded higher yields across fixed income assets, which put downward pressure on bond prices, including those of municipal bonds. Rapid shifts in investor sentiment, driven by those same geopolitical headlines, added to the volatility. Yields on a 10-year AAA general obligation bond, for example, climbed more than 60 basis points during March, hitting 3.11% on March 27—its highest level since September 2025. 2 At the same time, seasonal pressures worked against the municipal market. Investment flows declined as investors prepared to fulfill their April tax obligations. Meanwhile, new issuance remained high, and reinvestment capital dried up. All combined, these factors led to negative total returns for municipals, resulting in their weakest month in more than two years. 3

Tim: This dramatic rise in yields has had some repercussions for muni issuance, hasn't it?

Mark: It has. As we've discussed many times, March can be one of the trickier months for issuers due to weaker market technicals. Since supply outweighs demand, issuers need to offer higher yields in order to attract buyers. Now, we've had to add rate volatility from inflationary fears and geopolitical uncertainty into the equation, which has affected several new deals. Illinois and New York City were two examples of issuers having to reduce their deal sizes in late March. 4 In response, a major bank revised its 2026 municipal supply forecast down by roughly 6%, lowering it to $600 billion from a previous estimate of $640 billion. 4 It's worth noting that $600 billion would still be a year of record issuance, following last year's record total issuance of more than $585 billion. 5

data.title: Global PMI: Tracking The Sectors Hit Hardest By The Middle East War
data.description:
Global PMI survey data provide unique and timely insights into changing economic trends at the detailed sector level, helping policymakers and investors analyse the impact of events in near real time. In this respect, March’s PMI data provided the first insights into which sectors have been most affected by the outbreak of war in the Middle East. The data show that the economic impact on business growth was felt across all major industries, though consumer services – and tourism and recreation companies in particular – were hit the hardest.

Broad-based industry impact from war

Survey data underscored how the war in the Middle East has already had a material impact on economic growth around the world. Growth slowed worldwide to its lowest since last April, cooling sharply across both manufacturing and services in March, according to the PMI numbers produced by S&P Global on behalf of J.P. Morgan, hinting at a broad-based slowdown. Factory production growth hit a three-month low while the service sector reported its slowest rise in activity since November 2023.

Consumer services hit hardest

Drilling down deeper, the data showed weaker growth trends were recorded across all major industries globally. Only very modest expansions were consequently seen for basic materials, consumer goods and industrials (mainly comprising business services), and the recent upturn in healthcare growth ground almost to a halt. However, only the consumer services industry reported an actual drop in output, suffering its steepest downturn since November 2022.

The best performing industries were telecom services and technology, though even here rates of increase waned, the latter notably at its slowest since last May.

Financial services activity slows sharply

A particularly notable slowdown was also seen in financial services, reflecting financial market volatility and concerns over higher interest rates. Activity in the sector, which had been an important pillar of the global expansion in 2025, showed the smallest rise since November 2023.

Travel & tourism suffers steepest decline of all sub-sectors

At the more detailed sector level, the steepest drop in activity was recorded for tourism & recreation amid war-related travel concerns and higher fuel prices. The drop in tourism & recreation activity was the sharpest since January 2021, during the COVID-19 lockdowns in many countries.

However, output also fell sharply in the manufacture of construction materials and, to lesser extents, in metals & mining, real estate, beverages, transportation, healthcare services and basic material resources.

The decline in output in these eight sub-sectors during March contrasts with the universal rise in output reported across all 25 sectors covered by the PMI in February.

At the other end of the scale, the strongest-performing sub-sector in March was tech equipment, followed by ‘other’ (non-banking) financials and insurance, though growth rates of the latter two sectors slowed sharply compared to February.

data.title: S&P 500 Snapshot: Best Week Since November
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The S&P 500 snapped its seven-day win streak on Friday but still pulled out its second consecutive weekly gain, achieving its largest increase since November. The index climbed 3.9% from last week, a rally largely driven by optimism over potential geopolitical de-escalation, and is now 2.32% off its all-time high from January 27, 2026. Here is a snapshot of the index from the past week:

The table below summarizes the number of record highs reached each year dating back to 2013.

Here is a snapshot of the index from the past six months with a 50-day moving average:

S&P 500: A Perspective on Drawdowns

On October 9, 2007, the S&P 500 reached an all-time high, closing the day at 1565.15. Then on March 9, 2009, the index dropped ~57% off of its high from exactly 17 months before, closing the day at 676.53. This time period became known as the Global Financial Crisis. It took over 5 years before the index reached a new all-time high on March 28, 2013, where it closed out at 1569.19. The chart below is a snapshot of record highs and selloffs since the 2007 peak reached on October 9, 2007.

What happens if we take out the Global Financial Crisis? Here's a snapshot of the same chart above where the start date has been changed to the trough reached on March 9, 2009. Note the recent selloffs in 2022.

Here are a few tables with the number of days of a 1% or greater change in either direction and the number of days of corrections (down 10% or more from the record high).

And here is a linear chart of the index since October 9, 2007:

Here is a linearly scaled version of the same chart with the 50- and 200-day moving averages. The index has been above the 50-day moving average since April 8th, 2026, and above the 200-day moving average since April 8th, 2026. Additionally, the 50-day moving average has been above the 200-day moving average since July 1st, 2025.

S&P 500: A Perspective on Volatility

For a sense of the correlation between the closing price and intraday volatility, the chart below overlays the S&P 500 since 2007 with the intraday price range. On April 9th, 2025, the index experienced its largest intraday price volatility (10.77%) since December 24th, 2018 (19.10%). Also included is the 20-day moving average to identify trends in volatility. Over the past 20 days, the average percent change from the intraday low to the intraday high is 1.23%.

S&P 500 versus S&P Equal Weight

The S&P 500 is a market cap-weighted index which includes roughly the 500 largest U.S. stocks spanning 11 sectors. The S&P 500 Equal Weight Index includes the same constituents as the S&P 500, but each company is equally weighted at a fixed weight. So how do these two indexes match up against each other this year?

The S&P 500 is currently down 0.42% year-to-date, while the S&P Equal Weight is up 2.63% year-to-date.

ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include: iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPYM), and Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight ETF (RSP).

data.title: Perimeter Solutions: I'm Betting On One Hot Summer
data.description:
I previously reviewed Perimeter Solutions (PRM) in late 2025. I like that this small-cap business is a leader in a niche industry and that their new contract with the U.S. Forest Service is a positive sign for future growth.

Since that article was published, Perimeter is down roughly 6%, while the S&P 500 is flat. In 2026, Perimeter is down by more than 7%.

Despite this decline, I remain bullish on Perimeter because I believe we’re going to have a nasty wildfire season. It’s unseasonably warm in my neck of the woods, and I’ve heard from friends and relatives in Arizona and California that it’s already hit over 100 degrees.

This hearsay isn't science, but I’m going to get into why I think we're in for a rough summer with wildfires and why Perimeter will be in an excellent position to reap the rewards. Let’s dive in!

One Hot Summer

Let’s get into why I think this is going to be a rough wildfire season for Americans. To start, let’s look at the spring outlook (April through July 2026) from the National Interagency Fire Center (NIFC). The report noted that March precipitation was below the normal range for most of the United States, and it was well below normal in California, the Southwest, and the Great Basin. The below graphic illustrates this fact, and the western United States especially saw a lack of rainfall:

The report goes on to say these areas also had higher than normal temperatures. Forecasters from the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) are also predicting above-normal temperatures for the majority of the states during this same time frame.

So far in 2026, already 1.6 million acres of land have burned, which is more than double the previous ten-year average. Recently in Nebraska, over 800,000 acres of land burned due to three wildfires.

Next in the image below, you can see much of the United States has been in a drought, and things are getting worse in states like California, Oregon, and Arizona.

So just in the spring we have higher than normal temperatures (and forecasters expect this to continue), below normal precipitation, and drought that is present or expanding in much of the country. To me, that seems like a recipe for a brutal wildfire season.

Admittedly, the business has been trying to get more predictable in their cash flows, and the company’s five-year deal with the Forest Service helps in that respect. Yet much of the business is variable, especially when it comes to fire retardants. To me, it’s looking like despite more fixed revenue, Perimeter could be in a prime position to benefit should the United States have a rough wildfire season.

M&A - MMT Acquisition

In January 2026, Perimeter purchased Medical Manufacturing Technologies, LLC (MMT) for $685 million in cash. MMT is a leading provider for precision machinery associated with minimally invasive medical devices like catheters and guidewires.

On the surface, I don’t love the acquisition, as it seems like a perfect example of Peter Lynch’s “diworsification,” as this business doesn’t align with fire safety or fire-adjacent products.

On the latest earnings call, management even recognized this, as CFO Kyle Sable stated:

Our acquisition strategy is not industry-specific, it is strategy specific. What ties our businesses together is quality and the applicability of our operational value drivers, not whether a company is chemical, fire or safety by label. As a result, we expect future deals may come from new sub-verticals within the broader industrials landscape.

He went on to state Perimeter is looking for small businesses serving a niche that provide value to their customers who are trying to fix complex problems.

In a recent investor presentation, it was noted, MMT has been steadily growing their installed base, which is certainly a positive:

Still, this could prove to be a risky acquisition, and in general I feel like this is a small cap with some big risks, which I’ll discuss next.

Risks

Perimeter lists numerous items that might impact the business going forward. I’m going to touch on a few risks noted in the company’s Risk Factors section in their most recent 10-K.

As I alluded to above, Perimeter’s business is trying to shift towards more fixed, recurring revenue, but there is still considerable variability in Perimeter’s profits. Now the data provided by NOAA and NIFC tells you much of the United States is at risk for having wildfires given the weather conditions, but it’s not a sure thing these conditions will ultimately translate to the United States having more wildfires this year.

Perimeter also has some significant concentration risks. In 2025, two customers (the US Forest Service and BLM) accounted for 32% and 11% of the company’s total Fire Safety sales. In 2024, these same customers also accounted for 34% and 11% of the company’s total Fire Safety sales. Given Perimeter’s expertise in fire safety, I do believe they possess a moat in this niche industry, yet should the business lose either customer to an up-and-coming competitor, then this would surely spell trouble for the fire safety line of the business.

Perimeter’s management team has stated some capital will be allocated to M&A. There are numerous risks with this growth strategy, as the business could overpay, which could lead to impairment charges. Plus, I fear Perimeter is taking on far greater integration risks with acquisitions such as MMT, which aren’t bolt-on acquisitions.

Lastly, there is the company’s founder advisory agreement, which makes the finances more “messy,” as another analyst stated. I’ll get into this below, but the variable aspect of this agreement resulted in a $435 million expense to the income statement, which made the company’s operating loss for the year huge. Plus, as of 2025, the company still has $881.4 million payable to this agreement, which goes through 2031.

Financials

Before diving into the current 10-K filing, let’s zoom out and see how well Perimeter has performed on an annual basis over the last few years. As you can see below, revenue has grown over the last few years, and gross profit margins have improved as well:

In 2025, total revenue grew by 16%, with Fire Safety generating the majority of the company’s revenue. Fire Safety had sales of $489 million, which is a 12% increase compared to 2024. Special Products brought in roughly $164 million of revenue, which is a 31% increase compared to the prior year. Adjusted EBITDA was roughly $332 million for the year, which is an increase of 18% compared to 2024.

In 2025, the company’s fees associated with their founders advisory surged to $435 million, which resulted in large operating and net loss increases. To the investor unfamiliar with this agreement, it might appear the business is doing poorly, but given the variable component of the agreement, an increase in share value increases this fee, masking the company’s improved performance.

Moving on to the balance sheet, Perimeter does have a growing cash and cash equivalents balance compared to 2024, which is a positive, and the business has enough current assets to cover all of their current liabilities, as you can see below:

Still, the business has a large amount of long-term debt and fees payable associated with their founders advisory agreement.

Valuation

Perimeter has a valuation grade of a “C-” at Seeking Alpha:

I still think price-to-sales is likely the best valuation metric for this business, given Perimeter's been growing sales by double digits over the last few years.

While the company's price-to-sales ratio is higher than the sector median, it's declined compared to where it was a few months ago, as you can see below:

While higher than the sector median, I'd think this higher price-to-sales ratio is warranted given the likely growth in the upcoming year.

From another viewpoint, I have updated my reverse discounted cash flow model using the latest cash flow figures for Perimeter. Using a discount rate of 10% and a terminal rate of 3%, the business would need to continue to grow by 6% annually to justify today’s current stock price:

Analysts are expecting double-digit revenue growth in 2026 and then a drop back down to the single digits. Like these analysts, I’m expecting continued growth in 2026 due to raging wildfires, and I’d say 6% is very doable for this business. I think the business can generate greater than 6% assumed growth if wildfires continue to be bad over the next few years (which to me seems likely) and the business can successfully acquire and integrate new businesses into their overall portfolio.

Conclusion

Perimeter is a risky small-cap, no doubt. Despite the signs pointing to a hot summer with increasing temperatures and drought conditions intensifying in many areas of the United States, it’s not a sure thing this will translate into a rough wildfire season.

Furthermore, you have a complicated founders agreement, which makes the company look less attractive from a GAAP perspective. Then, with the cash the business is generating, they are making rather interesting M&A deals, which may or may not work out.

All that being said, Perimeter has a massive leg up on the competition in this niche market, and a summer rampant with wildfires would translate into considerable variable revenue. I’m an accountant, not a scientist, but the signs are all pointing to one hot and fiery summer, and I think that’s going to materialize. Growth investors willing to take on some risks should take a look at this small-cap, as I do believe Perimeter will end up doing very well in 2026.
